project.name: OneNexium
project.title: Phase 1.1 — Task & Goals Document
project.subtitle: Build the complete working platform engine. Quality comes in 1.2.

phase_definition.what_is_phase_1_1: Build a complete, working platform where every layer — auth, chat, AI generation, build pipeline, deployment, domains, backend gen — is connected and functional.
phase_definition.standard: It works. Not: it works beautifully. Not: it works fast. Not: it generates perfect code. It works. End-to-end. Reliably. For a real user session.
phase_definition.completion_statement: Phase 1.1 is complete when every engine is built and connected. A real user can go from signup to live web app. The web app may not be beautiful. The platform may not be fast. But it works.

the_one_test.description: A non-technical user who has never seen the platform before sits down with no help and completes the full journey independently.

steps.action: They open onenexium.com and sign up with Google.
They complete the onboarding flow — business type, name, brand.
They type: "Build me a complete website for my coaching business called Rise With Sarah".
They watch the generation happen in the chat. They see status updates.
A 4-page website appears in the preview iframe.
They type: "Add a contact form so clients can reach me".
The contact form is added. A database table is created. API route generated.
They click Go Live. The site deploys. A URL appears.
They open the URL in a browser. The site loads. The contact form works.
They go to Domains. They enter their own domain. DNS instructions appear.
They add the DNS record. The platform verifies it. SSL activates.
They invite a team member. That person signs in and sees the project.

the_one_test.pass_condition: If all 12 steps work without developer intervention, Phase 1.1 is complete.
the_one_test.note: The quality of the generated site is irrelevant to this test.

phase_1_1_build_it_working: MCP tools execute on real infrastructure
Build pipeline runs and reports errors
Docker image is built and pushed to ECR
Drizzle schema and API routes generated
phase_1_2_make_it_good: AI output quality is consistently high

goals.title: AWS Infrastructure — Fully Operational
goals.description: Every AWS service is running, connected, and verified. The foundation every other goal builds on.
tasks: VPC, subnets, security groups, route tables, NAT gateway — all created and verified
Platform EC2 (t3.large) running with all software installed: Node.js 20, Python 3.12, Docker, PM2, Caddy
Runtime EC2 (t3.xlarge) running with Docker Engine, Traefik, and onenexium-runtime Docker network
RDS PostgreSQL 16 running, accessible from Platform EC2, platform schema tables created
ElastiCache Redis 7.1 running, accessible from Platform EC2, ping returns PONG
S3 bucket created with correct folder structure and encryption
ECR repository created, Platform EC2 can push and pull images
ECS Fargate cluster created, task definition registered for build worker
ALB created, HTTPS listener active, ACM certificate issued and validated
All IAM roles created with correct permissions for each service
All secrets stored in Secrets Manager — no credentials in code or env files
CloudWatch alarms active for CPU, memory, storage thresholds
acceptance_criteria: SSH to Platform EC2 works. SSH to Runtime EC2 works.
psql connects to RDS from Platform EC2. SELECT 1 returns 1.
redis-cli -h $REDIS_ENDPOINT ping returns PONG.
aws s3 ls s3://onenexium-storage returns folder listing.
docker push to ECR succeeds from Platform EC2.
https://onenexium.com loads (even if 503 — SSL must be valid).
https://test.sites.onenexium.com loads with valid SSL via Traefik.

goals.title: Cloudflare + External Services — Fully Connected
goals.description: All services outside AWS are configured, verified, and storing their credentials correctly.
tasks: Cloudflare account created, domain added, nameservers pointing to Cloudflare
All DNS records created: onenexium.com → ALB, mcp.onenexium.com → Platform EC2, *.sites.onenexium.com → Runtime EC2
Cloudflare security settings configured: HTTPS always, TLS 1.3, HSTS, Full Strict SSL
Resend account created, domain verified, test email sends and receives
Anthropic API key active with spending limits set ($200 soft, $400 hard)
Google OAuth consent screen configured, OAuth credentials created
GitHub organisation created, 3 repositories created with CI/CD workflows
All external credentials stored in AWS Secrets Manager
.env.local generated for local development
acceptance_criteria: dig onenexium.com returns Cloudflare proxy IPs.
dig mcp.onenexium.com returns Platform EC2 static IP.
dig hello.sites.onenexium.com returns Runtime EC2 static IP.
Test email sent via Resend API is received in inbox.
Anthropic API test call returns a valid response.
Google OAuth client ID and secret are stored in Secrets Manager.
GitHub Actions CI/CD deploys on push to main for platform and MCP repos.

goals.title: Platform — Next.js App Running on EC2
goals.description: The Next.js platform application is deployed to Platform EC2, running under PM2, and accessible at onenexium.com.
tasks: Next.js 14 App Router project created with TypeScript, Tailwind CSS, shadcn/ui
Drizzle ORM configured with PostgreSQL — all platform schema tables created and migrated
PM2 process manager running Next.js — auto-restarts on crash, starts on EC2 reboot
GitHub Actions deploys on push to main branch — zero manual deployment steps
Health check endpoint /api/health returns { status: ok, db: connected, redis: connected }
ALB health check passes — Platform EC2 is healthy in target group
Basic dashboard page renders at /app (even if empty — it must load)
Environment variables loaded from AWS Secrets Manager at startup
404 and error pages exist — platform never shows raw Next.js error page
acceptance_criteria: https://onenexium.com loads and shows a real page (not default Next.js template).
https://onenexium.com/api/health returns { status: ok, db: connected, redis: connected }.
Push to main GitHub branch triggers deploy. Deploy completes without manual steps.
pm2 list on Platform EC2 shows onenexium-platform as online.
Kill PM2 process. PM2 automatically restarts it within 5 seconds.
Reboot Platform EC2. Platform comes back up automatically.

goals.title: Authentication — NextAuth Working End to End
goals.description: Users can sign up and sign in with Google and email magic link. Sessions persist. Protected routes redirect correctly.
tasks: NextAuth v5 (Auth.js) configured with Google OAuth provider
Email magic link provider configured via Resend
Drizzle adapter configured — users, accounts, sessions, verification_tokens tables populated on login
JWT sessions — role (admin/member/viewer) stored in token and exposed in session object
Middleware protecting /app/* routes — unauthenticated access redirects to /login
Login page at /login — clean UI with Google button and email input
Sign out flow — clears session, redirects to landing page
Session persists across browser tab close and reopen
Role-based access guard utilities (requireRole, useRole) implemented in lib/auth/rbac.ts
acceptance_criteria: Visiting /app without being logged in redirects to /login.
Clicking Google sign in completes OAuth and lands on /app.
A user row is created in RDS users table after first Google login.
After sign in, session.user.role is accessible in both server and client components.
Sending magic link email receives it in inbox. Clicking link signs in.
Signing out redirects to / and /app access redirects back to /login.

goals.title: Onboarding Flow — Working 4-Step Wizard
goals.description: New users complete a 4-step onboarding that creates their workspace and pre-fills their first AI generation prompt.
tasks: First-login detection — if user.onboarded is false, redirect to /onboarding after sign in
Step 1: Business type selection (Coaching / Consulting / Agency / Freelance / Other)
Step 2: Business name and description input
Step 3: Brand colour picker and logo upload (stored in S3)
Step 4: First project prompt setup — pre-fills chat with business description
Workspace record created in RDS on onboarding completion
User.onboarded set to true — never shown onboarding again
Progress indicator showing current step (1/4, 2/4, 3/4, 4/4)
Back button works on all steps — does not lose data
Onboarding state persists across refresh — user continues from last completed step
acceptance_criteria: New user after Google login lands on /onboarding.
Completing all 4 steps creates a workspace row in RDS.
Returning user who completed onboarding goes directly to /app — not /onboarding.
Logo uploaded in Step 3 is stored in S3 and URL saved in workspace record.
After Step 4, user lands in /app with chat pre-filled with their business description.

goals.title: Chat Interface — Message, Stream, and Display
goals.description: The chat interface sends messages to Claude, streams responses in real time, shows tool status updates, and displays the live URL when deployment completes.
tasks: Chat UI at /app/projects/[id] — left panel chat, right panel preview iframe
Text input at bottom — multi-line textarea, sends on Enter (Shift+Enter = new line)
Messages displayed: user messages right (indigo), AI messages left (surface card)
Streaming response — text appears character by character as Claude generates
Tool use status chips — show real-time operation labels as Claude calls MCP tools
Live URL card appears in chat after register_traefik_route returns success
Preview iframe auto-refreshes when deployment completes
Error display — if all retries fail, shows friendly message not raw error
Conversation history persists in RDS — reload page shows full history
Loading state — spinner shown while Claude API call is in flight
Mobile responsive — chat and preview stack vertically on small screens
acceptance_criteria: Typing a message and pressing Enter sends it. Streaming response appears within 2 seconds.
During generation, status chips appear: "Writing homepage...", "Building...", "Deploying..."
After full deploy, a clickable URL card appears in the chat.
Refreshing the page shows the full conversation history.
On mobile (375px width), the layout does not break.

goals.title: MCP Server — All 5 Namespaces Operational
goals.description: All 5 MCP server namespaces are deployed on Platform EC2, accessible via HTTPS, and every tool executes correctly.
tasks: FastMCP + FastAPI application deployed on Platform EC2 port 8000
Caddy reverse proxy serving HTTPS at mcp.onenexium.com → localhost:8000
JWT auth middleware validates Authorization Bearer token on every request
PM2 running uvicorn — auto-restart on crash, starts on EC2 reboot
GitHub Actions deploys MCP server on push to onenexium-mcp main branch
Structured logging to stdout captured by CloudWatch
acceptance_criteria: curl https://mcp.onenexium.com/health returns 200 with valid SSL.
curl to /codegen/write_file with valid auth token writes a real file to disk.
curl to /build/trigger_build launches a real ECS Fargate task.
curl to /deploy/start_container starts a real Docker container on Runtime EC2.
Invalid auth token returns 401 Unauthorized.
pm2 list shows onenexium-mcp as online.

goals.title: AI Generation Pipeline — Claude Generates Real Files
goals.description: Claude API is called correctly with all 5 MCP server URLs, Claude calls tools, tools write real files, and the generated code is valid Next.js that compiles.
tasks: Context assembly function — loads workspace, project state, file tree, last 10 messages
System prompt v1 — instructs Claude on Next.js 14 App Router, TypeScript, Tailwind, file writing order
Claude API call from /api/ai/generate — passes assembled context + conversation + 5 MCP server URLs
Streaming SSE response pipeline — Claude events piped to browser in real time
Tool use event parsing — write_file calls show "Writing filename..." chips in UI
Multi-page generation — Claude writes: layout.tsx, page.tsx, about/page.tsx, services/page.tsx, contact/page.tsx, Navbar.tsx, Footer.tsx
Generated code is valid TypeScript — tsc --noEmit passes without errors
Generated code uses Tailwind CSS utility classes — no inline styles, no custom CSS
Generated code uses shadcn/ui components — no raw HTML buttons or forms
Generated code is mobile responsive — uses Tailwind responsive prefixes
acceptance_criteria: Sending "Build me a website for a coaching business called Rise With Sarah" results in Claude calling write_file for at least 5 files.
All generated files are present at /data/projects/{id}/ on Platform EC2.
npm run build on the generated project exits with code 0 (no TypeScript errors).
80% of test prompts result in compilable code without retries.

goals.title: Build Pipeline — ECS Fargate Build Worker Operating
goals.description: Every build request launches an ECS Fargate task, builds the Next.js project, and reports success or structured errors back to the platform.
tasks: Build worker Docker image created and pushed to ECR
ECS Fargate task definition registered with correct IAM roles, memory, CPU
trigger_build MCP tool: creates build_job in RDS, syncs project files to S3, launches Fargate task
Build worker: downloads files from S3, overlays on base template, runs npm install + npm run build
Build worker: on success, runs docker build + docker push to ECR, updates RDS job status
Build worker: on failure, parses TypeScript errors into structured JSON, stores in S3, updates RDS
get_build_status MCP tool: polls RDS for job status, returns to Claude
get_build_errors MCP tool: returns structured errors with file path, line, message
Self-healing loop: Claude receives errors, calls write_file to fix, retriggers build — up to 3 attempts
Build logs stored in CloudWatch under /ecs/onenexium-build-worker
Fargate task terminates after completion — zero cost when idle
acceptance_criteria: trigger_build with a valid project launches a Fargate task visible in ECS console.
Fargate task completes and image appears in ECR repository.
Intentionally broken TypeScript file returns structured errors from get_build_errors.
Self-healing loop fixes a broken file and achieves a passing build on retry.
Build job status in RDS transitions: queued → building → complete.
Total build time from trigger to ECR push is under 5 minutes.

goals.title: Deployment Pipeline — Containers Live on Runtime EC2
goals.description: After a successful build, the Docker image is pulled on Runtime EC2, a container is started, Traefik routes the subdomain, and the site is live with SSL.
tasks: build_docker_image MCP tool: image already in ECR from build worker — records image URI in RDS
start_container MCP tool: SSH to Runtime EC2 via paramiko, docker pull from ECR, docker run with Traefik labels
Port assignment: sequential ports starting at 3001, stored in RDS, no conflicts
Traefik Docker labels: correct host rule, TLS enabled, letsencrypt cert resolver, service port
register_traefik_route MCP tool: polls the subdomain URL every 5 seconds for up to 90 seconds
Let's Encrypt certificate issued automatically by Traefik within 60 seconds
Platform EC2 updates RDS project.production_url after successful registration
Rolling deploy: new container started alongside old one, smoke test passes, old container stopped
Rollback: if smoke test fails, new container stopped, old container kept, user notified
Container health check: Traefik pings /api/health every 30 seconds
Container cleanup: old container stopped and removed on every successful rolling deploy
acceptance_criteria: After build completes, start_container results in docker ps showing user-app-{tenant_id} running on Runtime EC2.
https://{subdomain}.sites.onenexium.com loads in browser with valid SSL padlock.
The page served is the actual generated Next.js application.
Rolling deploy: update → new container → old container stopped — zero downtime.
Rollback: broken update → new container stopped → old container still serving traffic.

goals.title: Backend Code Generation — Drizzle + API Routes Working
goals.description: When the user requests backend features (contact form, bookings, newsletter), the AI generates Drizzle schemas and Next.js API routes, provisions the tenant database schema, and the features work.
tasks: provision_tenant_schema MCP tool: CREATE SCHEMA t_{tenant_id} in RDS, run Drizzle migrations
Backend feature detection in system prompt — Claude identifies form and interactive elements in frontend
Drizzle schema generation — contact form → contacts table, bookings → bookings table, newsletter → subscribers table
API route generation — POST /api/contact, GET+POST /api/bookings, POST /api/subscribe
Zod validation on every generated API route — invalid requests return 400 with structured errors
Email notification via Resend on contact form submission — sends to workspace owner email
create_s3_folder MCP tool: creates tenant folder in S3 for file uploads
store_secret MCP tool: stores per-tenant secrets in Secrets Manager
Generated API routes connect to correct tenant schema via DATABASE_URL search_path
Drizzle migration runs automatically in tenant schema after schema generation

backend_features.notification: Email via Resend to workspace owner

acceptance_criteria: User says "Add a contact form" — Claude generates lib/db/schema/contacts.ts and app/api/contact/route.ts.
Generated contacts table exists in t_{tenant_id} schema in RDS after provision_tenant_schema.
Submitting the contact form on the live site creates a row in the contacts table.
Contact form submission triggers an email to the workspace owner via Resend.
Submitting invalid data (empty email) returns 400 with error message.

goals.title: Custom Domain — DNS Verification + SSL Activation Working
goals.description: Users enter their own domain, receive DNS instructions, the platform polls for verification, and activates the domain with SSL.
tasks: Domain management page at /app/projects/[id]/domain
Domain input with format validation — only valid domain formats accepted
WHOIS-based or manual registrar detection — shows registrar-specific DNS instructions
CNAME record instructions: Type CNAME, Name, Value: {subdomain}.sites.onenexium.com, TTL 300
DNS polling: server-side job polls dns.resolve every 60 seconds after user submits domain
Domain record in RDS: status transitions from pending → verifying → verified
On DNS verified: call register_custom_domain MCP tool
register_custom_domain: SSH to Runtime EC2, update container Traefik labels to add custom domain host rule
Traefik auto-issues Let's Encrypt certificate for custom domain within 60 seconds
RDS domain.ssl_active set to true after certificate is confirmed
Email notification via Resend: "Your domain {domain} is now live with SSL"
Status UI: animated polling state → verified → SSL active (green badge)
HTTP → HTTPS redirect active on custom domain
acceptance_criteria: User enters test.theirdomain.com — DNS instructions appear with correct CNAME value.
After adding CNAME, platform detects it within 2 minutes of propagation.
https://test.theirdomain.com loads with valid SSL (not a self-signed cert).
http://test.theirdomain.com redirects to https://test.theirdomain.com.
User receives email notification when domain goes live.

goals.title: RBAC + Team Invites — Role Access Working
goals.description: Roles restrict what different users can see and do. Workspace owners can invite team members at specific roles.
tasks: Three roles defined and enforced: Admin (full access), Member (create/edit own projects), Viewer (read-only)
Middleware-level route protection — not just UI hiding, server validates role on every API call
Admin sees: all projects, team management page, delete buttons, billing section
Member sees: their own projects, can create new projects, cannot manage team
Viewer sees: projects list (read-only), cannot create projects, chat input is disabled, cannot edit
Role badge visible in platform sidebar under user avatar
Team management page at /app/settings/team — list members with role and joined date
Invite by email — sends magic link email with invitation token via Resend
Accept invite: GET /api/invites/accept?token={token} → creates user, assigns to workspace with specified role
Invite expires after 7 days — expired invites show error when accepted
Remove member: Admin can remove any member from workspace
acceptance_criteria: Viewer user: chat input box is disabled. "New Project" button is absent.
Member user: cannot see /app/settings/team page — redirected away.
Admin user: can see and use all features including team management and delete.
Invite email received. Clicking link creates account and joins workspace as member.
Expired invite (>7 days) shows "This invite has expired" when accessed.

goals.title: Visual Overlay Editor — Click-to-Edit Working
goals.description: Users in Edit Mode can hover over any section of their preview, click a bubble, describe a change, and the AI updates only that component.
tasks: Edit Mode toggle button in platform UI — switches preview from view-only to editable
In Edit Mode: hover over any section of preview iframe shows a floating edit bubble
Edit bubble contains: textarea for describing change, image upload button, Apply button
Component detection: iframe postMessage communication identifies which React component is hovered
Component mapped to source file and line range using file tree and component naming convention
On Apply: Claude is called with targeted context — current file content + user instruction
Claude uses apply_diff for targeted single-component edits — does not rewrite whole file
Build triggered after apply_diff — only affected file is changed
Preview refreshes showing the updated section within 3 minutes
Component undo: each component tracks last 3 versions — undo button in edit bubble
Exit Edit Mode: removes all edit bubbles, returns to clean preview view
acceptance_criteria: In Edit Mode, hovering over the hero section shows a floating bubble.
Typing "Change the headline to: Transform Your Life in 90 Days" and clicking Apply updates only the hero headline.
The rest of the page is unchanged after a component-level edit.
Undo button in the bubble reverts the hero to its previous state.

sprints.label: Sprint Zero
sprints.focus: Walking skeleton — one prompt to one live URL

sprints.label: Sprint 1
tasks: Complete Goal 3: Next.js platform deployed on EC2 with PM2 and CI/CD
Complete Goal 4: NextAuth Google + magic link working, sessions persisting, routes protected
Complete Goal 5: 4-step onboarding flow creating workspace, brand assets in S3
Platform sidebar layout with mobile responsive design
Projects list page showing created projects
Project creation flow — name → creates record → opens chat
Conversation history persists in RDS across page reloads
Health check endpoint active and passing ALB health checks

sprints.label: Sprint 2
sprints.focus: Complete MCP Server + Full Chat UI
tasks: Complete Goal 6: Full chat interface with streaming, tool chips, URL card, preview iframe
Complete Goal 7: All 5 MCP namespaces deployed with all tools responding correctly
Context assembly function — project state, workspace, file tree, conversation history
System prompt v1 written and tested on 20 diverse prompts
Claude API call correctly passes all 5 MCP server URLs
Tool event parsing — every MCP tool call shows a status chip in chat
Preview iframe embedded in right panel and refreshes on deploy
Error display — friendly messages when generation fails

sprints.label: Sprint 3
sprints.focus: AI Generation Quality + Build Pipeline Solid
tasks: Complete Goal 8: Multi-page generation working, 80% build success rate on test prompts
Complete Goal 9: ECS Fargate build worker running, self-healing loop executing correctly
System prompt iteration — fix top failure patterns from Sprint 2 testing
20-prompt test batch — record failures, fix system prompt, retest
Build worker Docker image deployed to ECR
Build job status machine working: queued → building → complete / failed
Structured TypeScript error parsing — line number, file, message
Self-healing loop: 3 retry attempts with Claude auto-fixing errors
Build logs visible in CloudWatch

sprints.label: Sprint 4
tasks: Complete Goal 10: Full deployment pipeline — container live on Runtime EC2 with SSL
Complete Goal 11: Drizzle schema + API routes generated, tenant schema provisioned, form submissions work
provision_tenant_schema creates correct PostgreSQL schema and runs migrations
Contact form end-to-end: user fills form → data in RDS → email sent via Resend
Rolling deploy working: new container, smoke test, old container stopped
Rollback working: failed smoke test keeps old container serving
Port assignment in RDS — no port conflicts under load
Container health check by Traefik every 30 seconds
Smoke tests: all page routes return 200, /api/health returns ok

sprints.label: Sprint 5
tasks: Complete Goal 12: DNS polling, Traefik custom domain routing, SSL issued, email notification
Complete Goal 13: Admin/Member/Viewer roles enforced at API level, team invite email flow
Domain management page with status badges
DNS polling job running server-side every 60 seconds
Registrar-specific DNS instructions displayed
register_custom_domain MCP tool adding Traefik host rules via SSH
Role enforcement on all API routes — not just UI hiding
Invite link creation, email sending, acceptance flow
Team management page: list members, invite, remove

sprints.label: Sprint 6
sprints.focus: Visual Overlay Editor + Full Integration Test
tasks: Complete Goal 14: Edit Mode toggle, hover bubbles, component detection, apply_diff edits
Component-level undo (last 3 versions)
Edit bubble image upload for visual reference
20-prompt final test batch — all prompts through full pipeline
Full user journey test: signup → onboard → generate → backend → deploy → domain → invite → edit
All 14 goals verified against acceptance criteria
Bug fix sprint: fix all High and Medium severity issues found in integration test
Phase 1.1 completion review — confirm every acceptance criterion passes
Phase 1.2 backlog defined and prioritised

acceptance_criteria_master.criterion: Both EC2 instances running. RDS, Redis, S3, ECR all accessible. ALB serving HTTPS with valid SSL.
onenexium.com, mcp.onenexium.com, and *.sites.onenexium.com all resolve to correct IPs.
Push to main branch deploys platform to EC2 automatically via GitHub Actions.
/api/health returns { status: ok, db: connected, redis: connected }.
New user signs up with Google → lands on /onboarding.
New user enters email → receives magic link → clicks it → lands on /app.
4-step onboarding completes, workspace row created in RDS, user.onboarded = true.
User who completed onboarding goes directly to /app on next login.
Unauthenticated visit to /app redirects to /login.
API route called with viewer session returns 403 for write operations.
Admin invites email → invite email received → clicked → user joins workspace as member.
curl https://mcp.onenexium.com/health returns 200 with valid SSL.
curl to MCP tool with no token returns 401. With valid token returns 200.
Claude calls write_file via MCP → file exists at /data/projects/{id}/app/page.tsx on Platform EC2.
Claude calls trigger_build → ECS Fargate task launches → visible in ECS console.
Valid Next.js project → build passes → image in ECR.
TypeScript error in generated code → get_build_errors returns [{file, line, message}].
Broken TypeScript file → Claude fixes → rebuild → build passes. All in one Claude session.
Prompt "Build a coaching website" → Claude writes at least 6 files including layout.tsx and 3 sub-pages.
Generated code from a typical prompt passes tsc --noEmit with zero errors.
After build: container starts on Runtime EC2, visible in docker ps.
https://{subdomain}.sites.onenexium.com loads in browser with valid SSL.
Edit → redeploy → old container stops, new container serves → zero downtime.
User asks for contact form → contacts table created in tenant schema → POST /api/contact inserts row.
Submitting contact form on live site inserts a row in RDS and sends email via Resend.
User enters custom domain → adds CNAME → platform detects → SSL active → site loads on custom domain.
In Edit Mode, hovering over a section shows bubble. Describing change updates only that component.
After visual edit, clicking Undo in bubble restores previous component version.
New user: signup → onboard → generate 4-page site → add contact form → deploy → connect domain → invite member. All steps work.
Reload chat page → full message history visible. Continue conversation → AI has context of prior messages.
After any generation or edit completes, preview iframe updates to show new state.

phase_1_2_backlog.reason: Revenue gating matters. But not before the engine works. Build it on a working product.
Quality is a system prompt problem. System prompt improves through iteration, not upfront design.

phase_1_2_backlog.item: LLM cost optimisation (semantic caching, model routing)
phase_1_2_backlog.reason: Premature optimisation. Understand real usage patterns before optimising.
Current 3-5 min is acceptable for Phase 1.1 testing. Optimise in 1.2.
Instrument when the system is stable. Not while building it.
Nice to have. Not needed for a working engine.
Templates improve quality. Quality is Phase 1.2.
Polling works. WebSockets are a performance improvement.
Only needed at 300+ concurrent connections. Not at Phase 1.1 scale.
S3 direct works fine at MVP. CDN is a cost/performance optimisation.
Users don't need analytics until they have a working product.
Testing is critical. But write tests for stable features, not changing ones.
Single region (ap-south-1) is fine for Phase 1.1.
Agency channel is Phase 2+. Focus on direct users first.
Build this in Phase 1.2 once you have data from real usage.

operating_rules.description: If a feature works but looks bad, it is done for Phase 1.1. Move on.
Do not start Goal N+1 until Goal N's acceptance criteria all pass.
Any idea that is not in the current sprint goes to BACKLOG.md. Not in the next sprint. BACKLOG. Until Phase 1.2.
Every day, run the full user journey from signup to a working page. If any step is broken, fix it before building new features.
Every time a goal's acceptance criteria pass, commit with a tag. git tag goal-07-complete. If something breaks later, you can roll back to this.
If either engineer is blocked for more than 2 hours, escalate. Blocked work is invisible waste.
Improving the system prompt is as important as writing code. Allocate 20% of each sprint to prompt testing and iteration.
Infrastructure and external services are the foundation. Any regression here takes priority over all feature work.

conditions: All goals scheduled for that sprint have their acceptance criteria passing.
The full user journey (signup → generate → deploy) still works end-to-end.
No new High severity bugs were introduced.
BACKLOG.md is updated with anything deferred.
The next sprint's goals are clear and agreed.
sprint_completion_definition.note: A sprint is NOT complete just because the tasks are checked off. The acceptance criteria are what matter — not the task list.

trigger_conditions: All 14 goals have their acceptance criteria passing
The complete 30-item acceptance criteria table (Section 04) all pass
The full 12-step user journey test (Section 01) passes without developer help
No High or Critical severity bugs are open
completion_review_process: Both engineers run the full user journey independently
All 30 acceptance criteria are checked off one by one
post_completion_actions: Tag the repository: git tag phase-1.1-complete

closing_statement: Phase 1.1 is the engine. Phase 1.2 is the polish. Build the engine first. Build it completely. Then make it brilliant.
